title: Suez Canal Grounding: More Friction in the Global Supply Chains
section_path: Suez Canal Grounding: More Friction in the Global Supply Chains
section_path_text: Suez Canal Grounding: More Friction in the Global Supply Chains
summary:
Main
By Ulf Bergman
Image reference: Source: Airbus Space | Source: Airbus Space | assets/2021-03-25_suez-canal-grounding-more-friction-in-the-global-supply-chains_img_suez_bfcd4046b3e5.jpg
Source: Airbus Space
The grounding of a 20,000 TEU container vessel in the Suez Canal, and the subsequent suspension of all traffic through the shortcut for shipments between Asia and ports in the Mediterranean and the Atlantic Basin, has highlighted its importance to world trade. Around twelve per cent of the global trade is passing through the canal annually, with on average 52 vessels transiting daily. Initial assessments by the canal authority suggested the incident was likely to be resolved within a few days, but with the suspension of traffic on its third day the Dutch salvage company SMIT is less optimistic and warns that it could take weeks to resolve the situation.
